title: Mid-infrared Assisted THz Phonon Amplification in a 2D Semiconductor for Room Temperature Detection
authors: Christopher Sumner, Jakob Ziewer, Anju Sajan, Fumin Huang, Rohit Chikkaraddy
abstract: Efficient and selective excitation of lattice vibrations is central to controlling energy flow at the nanoscale, yet remains challenging under conventional optical excitation. Here, we introduce a mid-infrared-assisted phonon amplification approach, termed MIRAPA, that enables efficient energy injection directly into vibrational bonds. Using surface-enhanced resonant Raman scattering in few-layer $\mathrm{MoS_2}$, we exploit strong exciton--phonon coupling to monitor phonon populations. When mid-infrared (MIR) light is introduced, it couples directly to out-of-plane lattice vibrations, leading to room-temperature phonon amplification exceeding $80\%$. Crucially, MIRAPA bypasses electronic excitation pathways, allowing the MIR power density to be nearly $300\times$ lower than that required for visible excitation to achieve comparable enhancement. The resulting phonon modulation is robust, persisting over more than $2800$ on/off cycles and exceeding $15$ hours of continuous-wave laser illumination without degradation. Quantitative analysis yields an effective noise-equivalent power of approximately $0.3\,\mathrm{nW}/\sqrt{\mathrm{Hz}}$ for MIR detection, highlighting the sensitivity of the approach. By combining vibrational selectivity, low-power operation, and long-term stability, MIRAPA provides a robust platform for probing and amplifying phonons in two-dimensional semiconductors. These results open new opportunities for nanoscale vibrational sensing, mid-infrared detection, and phonon-based coherent devices, including routes toward phonon lasing.
summary: This paper introduces a method called MIRAPA to selectively amplify THz phonons in MoS2 using mid-infrared light. This approach is much more energy-efficient than using visible light and avoids the heating issues typically associated with optical phonon excitation. The technique enables highly sensitive mid-infrared detection and provides a platform for future phonon-based coherent devices.
main_problem: Achieving efficient and selective excitation of lattice vibrations (phonons) in 2D semiconductors without the detrimental heating and decoherence caused by high-energy visible/NIR optical excitation.
main_result: Demonstrated mid-infrared assisted phonon amplification (MIRAPA) in MoS2, achieving over 80% amplification of the A1g phonon mode at room temperature with 300x lower power density than visible light.
method: The researchers used surface-enhanced resonant Raman scattering (SERRS) to monitor phonon populations in few-layer MoS2 on a gold foil, using a mid-infrared pump to drive phonons and a visible laser as a probe.
model_or_system: The experimental platform consists of few-layer MoS2 (approx. 5nm) exfoliated onto a 15nm thick gold (Au) foil, operating in a weak-coupling, anti-damping regime via exciton-mediated optomechanical coupling.
key_observables: Anti-Stokes/Stokes (aS/S) Raman intensity ratios, phonon occupation number (n), phonon linewidths (Gamma), and noise-equivalent power (NEP).
important_parameters: Room temperature operation, phonon amplification > 80%, effective NEP of ~0.3 nW/sqrt(Hz), and optomechanical coupling rate (hbar G) of 0.07-0.14 meV.
main_assumptions: The analysis assumes the phonon population follows a Bose-Einstein distribution and utilizes a Lorentzian field-enhancement model for optomechanical coupling.
figures_summary: Figures show the dependence of A1g and E2g1 phonon intensities on MIR and visible power, the modulation of the anti-Stokes signal under periodic MIR switching, and the efficiency comparison between MIR and visible pumping.
paper_structure: The paper introduces the MIRAPA concept, describes the experimental setup (2DoF geometry), details the Raman spectroscopy methodology, presents results on selective phonon amplification and efficiency, demonstrates long-term stability and sensitivity, and concludes with potential applications.
why_interesting: The paper demonstrates a highly efficient, non-thermal pathway for driving specific vibrational modes via exciton-mediated coupling, which is relevant to the study of driven-dissipative open quantum systems and optomechanics.
analysis_basis: full PDF text, analyzed in chunks

figures.caption: Figure 1: MIR excitation and THz-SERRS of MoS2 phonons. (a) Schematic of a monolayer MoS₂ crystal showing the trigonal prismatic coordination of Mo atoms (purple) sandwiched between two layers of S atoms (yellow). (b) Energy-level diagram illustrating optical transitions at the K/K′ valleys, giving rise to the A and B excitonic resonances (𝜀𝐴 and 𝜀𝐵). (c) Spectral schematic linking mid-infrared (MIR) excitation to visible optical readout. Left: MIR excitation at wavelength 𝜆MIR in the THz regime with the Raman active peaks indicated. Right: Reflectance (𝑅) spectrum at 105K normalised to the background reflectance from the gold substrate in the visible regime showing the 𝜀𝐴 and 𝜀𝐵...

Figure 2: Temperature-dependent MIR-induced phonon amplification. (a) Stokes and anti-Stokes maps of MoS2 for various sample temperatures without MIR illumination. (b) Differential SERRS maps (ΔSERRS) obtained under MIR illumination with power density, 𝑃𝜆MIR = 3.60µW/µm2. (c) Percentage change in the anti-SERRS intensity for selected phonon modes as a function of sample temperature. (d) MIR-induced phonon amplification from the thermal population estimated by Eq(5) for A1g, E2g

Figure 3: MIR and visible power dependence of anti-Stokes SERRS. (a) Differential anti-Stokes SERRS spectra for increasing MIR power density 𝑃λMIRat fixed visible excitation power 𝑃λL=280µW/µm2. Each spectrum is normalised to the maximum intensity obtained in the absence of MIR illumination, vertically offset for clarity, and labelled with the corresponding MIR power density. (b) ΔSERRS 𝑎𝑆 spectra for increasing 𝑃λLwith no MIR illumination (𝑃λMIR=0). Each spectrum is normalised to its own maximum intensity and labelled with illuminated 𝑃λL. (c, d) The 𝑎𝑆 percentage change in the ΔSERRS intensity extracted from a and b respectively for the A1g and E2g
